event: The Nativity — Adoration of the Christ Child in the stable
symbols: infant on the ground — the humility of the Incarnation, God laid low
kneeling Mary and Joseph — adoration of the divine made flesh
ox and donkey — creation recognizing its Creator
star in the sky — divine sign guiding the world to the Savior
wooden stable — the poverty into which the King of Kings was born
shepherds approaching — the lowly and humble as the first to receive the Good News
description: A wide predella-format painting depicting the Nativity. Under a simple wooden shelter open on all sides, the infant Jesus lies on the ground on a white cloth. The Virgin Mary kneels in prayer to the left, wearing a blue mantle. Joseph sits in contemplation to the right, in red robes. An ox and donkey stand behind a wooden railing at center. Shepherds approach from both sides — one kneeling on the left, another walking with a staff on the far right. A small angel hovers in the sky above, and a golden star shines. A rolling green landscape extends to distant hills under a clear sky.
theological_description: The Nativity of Christ is the Incarnation made visible — the moment when 'the Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us' (John 1:14). The infant laid upon the bare ground rather than a royal cradle expresses the kenosis described by Paul: Christ 'emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men' (Philippians 2:7). Mary's prayerful adoration fulfills her own prophecy that 'all generations will call me blessed' (Luke 1:48), while Joseph's contemplative presence reflects his role as the just guardian chosen by God (Matthew 1:20). The shepherds, first recipients of the angelic announcement (Luke 2:8-12), represent God's preferential love for the humble — 'He has brought down the mighty from their thrones, and exalted those of humble estate' (Luke 1:52). The ox and donkey, drawn from Isaiah 1:3 — 'The ox knows its master, the donkey its owner's manger, but Israel does not know, my people do not understand' — offer a gentle rebuke: even beasts recognize their Creator, while many in Israel did not receive Him (John 1:11). The star fulfills Balaam's ancient prophecy: 'A star will come out of Jacob; a scepter will rise out of Israel' (Numbers 24:17).

scripture_refs.reason: The birth of Jesus and His being laid in a manger — the direct narrative depicted
The shepherds receiving the angelic announcement and coming to adore the infant
The Word became flesh — the theological reality of the Incarnation made visible
Christ emptying Himself and taking the form of a servant — the humility of the stable birth
The ox and donkey knowing their master — the animals present at the manger
The star rising out of Jacob — the star shining above the Nativity scene
